title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
summary:
Hercule Poirot confronts Jane Hobbs, accusing her and Samuel Kid of conspiring to frame Nancy Duane for the murders of Harriet Sipper, Ada Gransberry, and Richard Nigges at the Blaxland Hotel. Poirot argues that the evidence against Nancy is illogical and too convenient. He notes that while the killer supposedly panicked and dropped two keys in front of Samuel Kid, the killer had previously demonstrated a calm, calculated manner by leaving a note at the front desk announcing the murders with the numbers "121, 238, 317." Furthermore, Poirot points out that Nancy Duane has a solid alibi, having had dinner with Mrs. Louisa Wallace.

Poirot identifies striking similarities between the hotel murders and a previous incident at the Browns Hotel involving a woman named Miss Jean. In both cases, the perpetrator acted as an agent of justice against victims who appeared to deserve death, and both crimes provided an excessive amount of specific information to the police, such as the motive for retribution and the detail of placing cufflinks in the victims' mouths.

Poirot reveals that Jane Hobbs targeted him at "The Happy Café" because she knew of his reputation as a meticulous former European police officer with connections to Scotland Yard. He asserts that her terrified demeanor at the café was a calculated performance intended to manipulate him into investigating the case and helping her frame Nancy Duane. Finally, Poirot questions how Jane could have known the specific, unusual detail regarding the placement of cufflinks in the victims' mouths unless she was involved in the planning.
